text: The laparoscope enters the abdomen, revealing a large perforated pre-pyloric gastric ulcer. There is visible contamination of gastric material into the abdominal cavity, contributing to significant peritonitis and the presence of intra-abdominal fluid.

The surgeon performs primary closure of the perforated pre-pyloric gastric ulcer using multiple intra-optic sutures with an intracorporeal suturing technique. The closure is reinforced with a second layer of sutures.

The surgeon drains the intra-abdominal fluid and irrigates the abdomen with an antibiotic solution. The omental attachments to the transverse colon are mobilized to create an omental patch. The integrity of the closure is confirmed by inflating the stomach with diluted blue dye via a nasogastric tube, showing no dye extravasation. The area is then reinforced and patched with the greater omentum.
